uses: Haea massonii, a plant species native to Australia, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The leaves and stems of the plant contain compounds that have been reported to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Traditionally, Haea massonii has been used to treat skin conditions, such as wounds and eczema, as well as to aid in the healing process of various other ailments. However, further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of the plant's medicinal properties. As with any traditional remedy, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Haea massonii for medicinal purposes.
parts:
Haeckelia or Haemacs massonii is actually another name for Haemadipsa  or  Haeckelia  massonii, but the correct common name for the species is Haemadipsa zeylanica. Haemadipsa zeylanica. 
Haeckelia massonii (also known as the Indian leech) is a species of leech, not a plant. 
The medicinal parts of this leech are the salivary glands which produce a compound called hirudin, which is used to dissolve blood clots and is useful in preventing thrombosis. 
The hirudin extracted from Haemadipsa zeylanica's salivary glands is used medicinally to treat conditions such as deep vein thrombosis. 
Research on hirudin has also shown potential for use in treating conditions such as heart failure and stroke.
